summary.title: Houston Energy Corridor — Multi-Family & Commercial Market Q2 2026
summary.narrative: The Houston Energy Corridor submarket experienced significant headwinds through 2020–2024 driven by oil price volatility and remote work adoption; however, multi-family residential demand has proven resilient, supported by in-migration and energy sector hiring recovery. The office segment remains challenged with ~18% vacancy. For the dormitory complex at 1720 Eldridge Pkwy, the relevant benchmark is Houston multi-family residential — where West Houston vacancy is a tight 5.2% and rent growth of +2.1% YoY has been recorded. The complex's subsidised pricing ($450/mo) is significantly below market ($950/mo average), reflecting its employee welfare purpose rather than investment optimisation.
keyThemes: Houston multi-family vacancy remains tight at 5.2% in West Houston submarket
Energy Corridor office market structurally challenged — ~18% vacancy creating downward pressure on rents
GoWell employee housing significantly below market ($450 vs. $950 market rate)
Houston economy benefits from LNG export boom and energy transition capex
Refinancing market tighter — US commercial lenders applying conservative LTVs on subsidised-use assets

strengths: Fully occupied corporate-use dormitory — captive GoWell employee tenant base
80 units with amenities (pool, gym, community room) competitive with Class B/C multi-family
Below-market rents ($450 vs. $950) create strong employee retention value to GoWell US
Freehold ownership in improving West Houston submarket
weaknesses: Subsidised rent profile ($450/mo) suppresses cap rate to 1.28% vs. 6.0% market cap rate
Lenders unable to underwrite to market rents due to contractual below-market use
Aging HVAC infrastructure requires $185K Phase 2 replacement
Low DSCR (0.09x) creates refinancing challenge — insufficient income to service market-rate debt
opportunities: Convert to market-rate on future lease with new GoWell corporate tenancy agreement at $700/mo (market-approach)
Sell to regional multi-family investor at ~$8.5M (6% cap rate on market rents) — proceeds could reduce debt
Houston LNG sector expansion may drive GoWell headcount growth, improving occupancy to 95%+
threats: Regions Bank may not refinance at favourable terms given subsidised use — risk of forced sale
Interest rate environment remains elevated — refinancing at 5.5–6.0% adds $26–$35K/yr to debt service
Harris County tax reassessment could increase property tax if value appreciated

comparables.address: Greenway Commons, 1600 Dairy Ashford Rd, Houston TX
comparables.notes: Class C multi-family; 84 units; West Houston submarket; market rents $920/mo avg

comparables.address: Briar Village, 1800 Eldridge Pkwy, Houston TX
comparables.notes: Older Class C; directly comparable location to subject; some deferred maintenance

comparables.address: Park West Apartments, 1900 Barker Cypress, Houston TX

marketRisks.title: Lender Scrutiny of Subsidised Use
marketRisks.description: Refinancing lenders will apply market cap rate but then haircut for below-market income — may require 30% LTV or less, implying $3.0M max loan vs. current $4.1M balance.

marketRisks.title: Houston Energy Sector Cyclicality
marketRisks.description: GoWell headcount (and dormitory demand) tied to Houston energy sector; oil price downturn could reduce occupancy.

marketOpportunities.title: Market-Rate Conversion Option
marketOpportunities.description: Converting dormitory to market-rate ($850–$950/mo) would generate $816K–$912K gross annual income vs. current $351K — transforming the asset economics and enabling conventional refinancing.

marketOpportunities.title: Houston LNG Expansion Tailwind
marketOpportunities.description: US LNG export capacity expansion driving energy company hiring in Houston — GoWell Americas headcount growth supports sustained dormitory demand.

marketImplication.title: Refinancing is Existential — Act Before June 30
marketImplication.body: The August 2026 maturity of the Regions Bank facility is the single most time-critical item in the entire portfolio. The Houston multi-family market is healthy enough to support refinancing, but the subsidised-use model creates a structural challenge in the lender underwriting process. Two parallel tracks are recommended: (1) negotiate a 3-year extension with Regions Bank while preparing the refinancing package, and (2) brief 2–3 alternative lenders on the corporate-use model with a path to higher rents. If neither achieves results by July 31, bridge financing should be initiated immediately to avoid default.
